I agree with all your comments in this review except for the first part of this one (although I'll agree the cd is a bit cumbersome). Even though it might be easy to lookup a tox tutorial and figure out how to run tests and then determine how to transfer the commands learned in the tutorial to your own project, I think its immensely helpful to have a copy-pastable command line in a python source file that tells you how to run it. 

I just spent some time reworking some of this PR into a new one. I don't use tox on a regular basis, so I forgot what the exact command was to run the tests. The fact that I came back here, found this note, and didn't have to context switch and wonder "how is this done again?", is a working example for why  having a docstring in your test file (rather than something you have to go searching for in the docs) that tells you how to run it is a very worthwhile .

benched a few methods (yours, izip, izip + imap, all(imap(izip)), etc). memory utilization is the same once you pull in generators.......seems that sticking to izip is the fastest, and is the closes to the native "list == list" check...now, assuming that self.records == other.records is not feasible (wondering why)... 
I tested with 1M records, plus a different record placed at the very end. 
map() is ~25x slower than list==list, and 3x memory hungry
izip is ~9x slower, 1x memory
all(imap(izip)) is ~16x slower , 1x memory
So, here the "izip" version, that is quite readable, too
```
from itertools import izip
....
    if len(self.records) != len(other.records):
        return False
    for x in izip(self.records, other.records):
        if x[0] != x[1]:
            return False
    return True
```

my understanding is that we mostly run backfill with local scheduler which spin off couples of worker multiprocess to run the task. Once we set the task state to failed, the task will get rerun next time(using clear first, or with --rerun_failed_task). I spent all day trying to figure it out and reading the code of django and I think it's not possible to handle the exception of a middleware when this one is thrown during `process_request`. 

Indeed when [loading middlewares](https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.10/django/core/handlers/base.py#L78), django [wraps the `get_response` method to handle exceptions](https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.10/django/core/handlers/exception.py#L36-L43). But we are using the `MiddlewareMixin` class that [calls `process_request` before `get_response`](https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.10/django/utils/deprecation.py#L122-L124). So if an error occurs during `process_request`, the programs just crashes.

@jborean93 So by default requests calls into urllib3, and gets back a urllib3 `HTTPResponse` object that ends up in `Response.raw`. However, there is no *requirement* that that be the call, and frequently users try a different backend or put a different file object there (e.g. `BytesIO`). For that reason, Requests core projects must not unconditionally assume that `Response.raw` is a urllib3 `HTTPResponse` object. They may attempt to use it like one, but if they fail they should fall back to a sensible behaviour.

So I'm saying we should look in those places first, but if those places don't exist we should tolerate that rather than throwing exceptions.

There is an overall deadline for the stream but keep in mind that if the stream gets re-started then so does the deadline calculation - that part is separate from the deadline we're talking about here.

There's actually (lol) three separate retries at play here:

1. There's the retry-wrapped method to *establish* the stream. This has a deadline set by gapic.
2. There's the gRPC timeout which is set when the stream is established. This is the "overall stream deadline" you're thinking of. This is also set by gapic.
3. There's now this retry for re-establishing the stream. You can see this as sitting at a level above the other two things- it doesn't care or know about those settings. It just tries to re-open the stream if the iterator breaks. For sanity if has a deadline on retrying the stream on a single failure- if it succeeds in re-establishing the stream the deadline is no longer in play and is reset if the iterator breaks again after being re-established. In all likelyhood the deadline will never even be a factor as the retry in (1) should be able to establish a connection. The deadline is just there to keep a degenerate case from causing the app to block indefinitely.

```python
        if client_info is None:  # create a new instance
```

```
Object.retrieve(488353213969592764)
```

```
Object.all(path='/1000G')
Object.all(path='/1000G', vault_id=7205)
Object.all(path='/1000G', vault_name='public')
```

Search functionality in the API is presently built on top of the Logical Object model.  Accordingly, searching within the client can be performed by the correct `Object.all(query=xxx)` call:

```
Object.all(query='GRC', vault_id=5906)
```

Yes, that's right, the reason why I did this is because the scoping commands `%% <whatever>` would not apply once the scope is changed, so at the very least, they should not be saved.

The other option would be to match for different scenarios and save accordingly. i.e., scope commands are not saved, cli commands have the scope appended, non-azure cli commands (using `#`) are saved alone, etc.
In addition, inside a scope, some history is no longer valid. i.e. all commands from other scopes. Thus, we would also need to parse through the history if all scopes use the same source.
This can be confusing, a user may wonder why some things are showing up, some aren't, and why some differ from what they typed.

To me, having a simpler rule defined, where scoping creates a different "environment" of sorts with its own history seems much more intuitive. Once that environment is left, the history is cleaned up and gone.

I added that if statement probably because it doesn't make logical sense to say `--db=sqlite::memory:`. We will need in-memory SQLite in cases where `--logdir` is passed, but not `--db`.

---

**Commentary unrelated to this change**

I'll likely send a PR soon changing the way we think about the DB flag. After carefully studying the SQLite C API, I think the concept of a "SQLite URI" is a mistake, for many reasons. 

Firstly, the SQLite C API, if one actually chooses to use its "URI API" it then uses `file:` as the scheme. Yet here and in many other frameworks, `sqlite:` or `sqlite3:` have been used as its scheme. I believe the URI scheme for SQLite should be empty/null.

Secondly, a plain old path IS a an RFC 3986 compliant URI. Proof:

```pycon
>>> import urlparse
>>> urlparse.urlparse('/hello')
ParseResult(scheme='', netloc='', path='/hello', params='', query='', fragment='')
```

Thirdly, I believe it will be far wiser for us to use environment variables for SQLite tuning, so long as they're only used for tuning. See our [sqlite.cc](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/febdd26ae594133d24f82544706b1e012a5cf1ea/tensorflow/core/lib/db/sqlite.cc#L135).

That's the theory, and it works for relpro tasks, which create `task-graph.json` with an accurate `taskId` and task definition.

Retriggers seem to create a `task-graph-0.json`, which isn't supported by CoT verification... the reasoning seems to be that retriggers allow for multiple graphs (e.g. retrigger graph 0-5). This means CoT verification will fail.

However, fuzzy task matching only worked for a single task on current retriggers. Any subsequent tasks failed, e.g. a retriggered signing task might pass CoT verification, but a child beetmover task would fail. Fuzzy task matching adds time, log spam, and doesn't fully solve the problem, so we're leaning towards saying "retriggers aren't compatible with CoT", or "retriggers should be for tests only".

I'd like to avoid hardcoding the service endpoints here. Is there any chance we can make `getEndpointOnWindows` implement a similar logic to `getEndpointOnLinux`? Can you elaborate a bit why the existing `getEndpoint(...)` method doesn't work under Windows? Thanks

If the same approach (calling a python process that reads the configuration) does not work under Windows, another option to get the default service ports/endpoints would be to read the `localstack_client` config file from the installation directory - typically `$TMPDIR/localstack_install_dir/.venv/lib/python*/site-packages/localstack_client/config.py` (where `python*` is a placeholder for the concrete python version, for example `python2.7` or `python3.6`).

The problem with using the default ports, though, is that this could lead to conflicts if users provide configuration settings that specify custom ports per service. Therefore, the dynamic port lookup (by calling the python process) would be the preferred option here.
